perturbation.original_text: 11. PAYMENT: Under Letter of Credit or T/T: Under the Letter of Credit: The Buyer shall open an irrevocable letter of credit with the bank within 30 days after signing the contract, in favor of the Seller, for 100% value of the total contract value. The letter of credit should state that partial shipments are allowed. The Buyer's agent agrees to pay for the goods in accordance with the actual amount of the goods shipped. 80% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.1. The remaining 20% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.2. The Letter of Credit shall be valid until 90 days after the latest shipment is effected. Under the T/T The trustee of the buyer remitted the goods to the seller by telegraphic transfer in batches as agreed upon after signing each order.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. PAYMENT: Under Letter of Credit or T/T: Under the Letter of Credit: The Buyer shall open an irrevocable letter of credit with the bank within 30 days after signing the contract, in favor of the Seller, for 100% value of the total contract value. The letter of credit should state that partial shipments are allowed. The Buyer's agent agrees to pay for the goods in accordance with the actual amount of the goods shipped. The Letter of Credit shall be valid until 90 days after the latest shipment is effected. Under the T/T The trustee of the buyer remitted the goods to the seller by telegraphic transfer in batches as agreed upon after signing each order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific payment percentages (80% and 20%) tied to document stipulations in clauses 12.1 and 12.2 respectively, it introduces ambiguity around how the payment will be executed. This creates an in-text contradiction as the contract still references Clause 12.1 and 12.2 in relation to documentation requirements for payments, but the specific payment allocation based on these documents is omitted. This omission will affect the enforcement of the payment terms because there is a lack of clear definitions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.2 (1) Invoice in 3 originals indicating contract number and L/C number. (2) Final acceptance certificate signed by the Buyer and the Seller.
perturbation.justification: The omission of specific payment percentages (80% and 20%) tied to documentation stipulations in clauses 12.1 and 12.2 respectively introduces ambiguity about how the payment will be executed. Clause 12.2 states '(2) Final acceptance certificate signed by the Buyer and the Seller.' The original text clearly defines what percentage of the shipment value will be paid against this document. Removing the payment percentages in clause 11, while maintaining the reference to clause 12.2 in that same clause, creates a contradiction. The altered text creates an in-text contradiction as the contract references Clause 12.2 in relation to documentation requirements for payments, but the specific payment allocation based on these documents is omitted, making the obligations unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 16. CLAIMS: Within 7 days after the arrival of the goods at destination, should the quality, specification, or quantity be found not in conformity with the stipulations of the Contract except those claims for which the insurance company or the owners of the vessel are liable, the Buyers, on the strength of the Inspection Certificate issued by the China Commodity Inspection Bureau, have the right to claim for replacement with new goods, or for compensation, and all the expenses (such as inspection charges, freight for returning the goods and for sending the replacement, insurance premium, storage and loading and unloading charges etc.) shall be borne by the Sellers. The Certificate so issued shall be accepted as the base of a claim. The Sellers, in accordance with the Buyers' claim, shall be responsible for the immediate elimination of the defect(s), complete or partial replacement of the commodity or shall devaluate the commodity according to the state of defect(s). Where necessary, the Buyers shall be at liberty to eliminate the defect(s) themselves at the Sellers' expenses. If the Sellers fail to answer the Buyers within one weeks after receipt of the aforesaid claim, the claim shall be reckoned as having been accepted by the Sellers.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. CLAIMS: Within 7 days after the arrival of the goods at destination, should the quality, specification, or quantity be found not in conformity with the stipulations of the Contract except those claims for which the insurance company or the owners of the vessel are liable, the Buyers have the right to claim for compensation. If the Sellers fail to answer the Buyers within one weeks after receipt of the aforesaid claim, the claim shall be reckoned as having been accepted by the Sellers.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrases such as "on the strength of the Inspection Certificate issued by the China Commodity Inspection Bureau",  "all the expenses (such as inspection charges, freight for returning the goods and for sending the replacement, insurance premium, storage and loading and unloading charges etc.) shall be borne by the Sellers", "The Certificate so issued shall be accepted as the base of a claim", "The Sellers, in accordance with the Buyers' claim, shall be responsible for the immediate elimination of the defect(s), complete or partial replacement of the commodity or shall devaluate the commodity according to the state of defect(s). Where necessary, the Buyers shall be at liberty to eliminate the defect(s) themselves at the Sellers' expenses", a significant portion of the claims process is removed which leaves it up to interpretation on how the claims should be made which creates an in-text contradiction. While the clause still allows buyers to claim compensation, the omission of the need of an Inspection Certificate, that would validate the claim, removes the verifiable process for claims, thus affecting enforcement because there is no verifiable claim process to enforce the compensation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16. CLAIMS: Within 7 days after the arrival of the goods at destination, should the quality, specification, or quantity be found not in conformity with the stipulations of the Contract except those claims for which the insurance company or the owners of the vessel are liable, the Buyers have the right to claim for compensation. If the Sellers fail to answer the Buyers within one weeks after receipt of the aforesaid claim, the claim shall be reckoned as having been accepted by the Sellers.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for an Inspection Certificate and the details of the claim process, including who bears the expenses. The contradicted text expects a process for claims to be validated, but the changed text provides no method of verifying the claim, making enforcement unclear as it's based solely on the buyer's claim for compensation. The omission of the Inspection Certificate, which served as the 'base of a claim' in the original text, now contradicts the contradicted text because it removes the verifiable process, creating a strong inconsistency. This is a strong in-text contradiction since the contradicted text expects a verifiable process, while the changed text removes it. This adheres to the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 18. LATE DELIVERY AND PENALTY: Should the Sellers fail to make delivery on time as stipulated in the Contract, with exception of Force Majeure causes specified in Clause 17 of this Contract, the Buyers shall agree to postpone the delivery on condition that the Sellers agree to pay a penalty which shall be deducted by the paying bank from the payment. The penalty, however, shall not exceed 5% of the total value of the goods involved in the late delivery. The rate of penalty is charged at 0.5% for every seven days, odd days less than seven days should be counted as seven days. In case the Sellers fail to make delivery ten weeks later than the time of shipment stipulated in the Contract, the Buyers have the right to cancel the contract and the Sellers, in spite of the cancellation, shall still pay the aforesaid penalty to the Buyers without delay, the seller should refund the money received and pay the 30% of the total goods price of the penalty
perturbation.changed_text: 18. LATE DELIVERY AND PENALTY: Should the Sellers fail to make delivery on time as stipulated in the Contract, with exception of Force Majeure causes specified in Clause 17 of this Contract, the Buyers shall agree to postpone the delivery on condition that the Sellers agree to pay a penalty which shall be deducted by the paying bank from the payment. The penalty, however, shall not exceed 5% of the total value of the goods involved in the late delivery. In case the Sellers fail to make delivery ten weeks later than the time of shipment stipulated in the Contract, the Buyers have the right to cancel the contract and the Sellers, in spite of the cancellation, shall still pay the aforesaid penalty to the Buyers without delay, the seller should refund the money received and pay the 30% of the total goods price of the penalty
perturbation.explanation: By removing "The rate of penalty is charged at 0.5% for every seven days, odd days less than seven days should be counted as seven days", the clause creates ambiguity around how the penalty is calculated, thus creates an in-text contradiction. While a penalty is still mentioned, there's now no clarity on the rate or calculation method for delays less than ten weeks. This affects enforcement, creating uncertainty in the amount to be paid for late delivery.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 18. LATE DELIVERY AND PENALTY: Should the Sellers fail to make delivery on time as stipulated in the Contract, with exception of Force Majeure causes specified in Clause 17 of this Contract, the Buyers shall agree to postpone the delivery on condition that the Sellers agree to pay a penalty which shall be deducted by the paying bank from the payment. The penalty, however, shall not exceed 5% of the total value of the goods involved in the late delivery. In case the Sellers fail to make delivery ten weeks later than the time of shipment stipulated in the Contract, the Buyers have the right to cancel the contract and the Sellers, in spite of the cancellation, shall still pay the aforesaid penalty to the Buyers without delay, the seller should refund the money received and pay the 30% of the total goods price of the penalty
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific penalty rate (0.5% for every seven days), while the contradicted text, which is the original clause itself, expects a clear method for calculating the penalty. This omission introduces ambiguity regarding the penalty calculation for delays less than ten weeks, creating a direct contradiction. The original clause defines a concrete method which is now missing, thus creating an in-text contradiction within the document because the section contradicts itself as the means of calculating the penalty is now not defined. This adheres to the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.
